name: HTTPX
what_it_does: Full-featured async and sync Python HTTP client with HTTP/2 support, connection pooling, and a requests-compatible API.
use_cases: Making concurrent async HTTP requests to external APIs from an agent pipeline
Streaming large response bodies without buffering the entire payload in memory
Reusing HTTP/2 connections to reduce latency across repeated calls to the same host
Configuring per-request or client-level timeouts to prevent agent hangs
Mounting custom transports (e.g. WSGI/ASGI) for in-process testing of HTTP integrations
not_for: WebSocket connections (use websockets or aiohttp instead)
Production gRPC communication (use grpcio stubs instead)
Scraping at massive scale where a dedicated crawler framework is more appropriate
best_when: You need a single client that works in both sync and async contexts and want HTTP/2 multiplexing or precise timeout control.
avoid_when: Your codebase already standardises on requests and you have no async requirements — the added dependency is unnecessary.

auth.notes: Library — auth is implemented by the application via headers, query params, or custom Auth classes.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Raises typed exceptions (ConnectTimeout, ReadTimeout, HTTPStatusError) that are easy to catch and classify; stack traces identify the exact transport layer.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Stateless per-request; safe methods (GET, HEAD) are inherently idempotent. Retry logic must be implemented by the caller.
known_agent_gotchas: follow_redirects defaults to False — agents hitting redirect-heavy APIs must pass follow_redirects=True explicitly or requests will fail silently with a 3xx response
Timeout parameter accepts a Timeout object or a float; passing a single float sets connect+read+write+pool timeouts identically, which may cause pool exhaustion under high concurrency
AsyncClient must be used as an async context manager or explicitly closed — leaking it produces ResourceWarning and unclosed connector errors in long-running agents
HTTP/2 requires the optional h2 extra (pip install httpx[http2]); omitting it silently falls back to HTTP/1.1 without any warning
Cookies are not persisted across Client instances by default; agents needing session cookies must reuse the same Client object

security_score_components.security_notes: Verifies TLS certificates by default; verify=False disables verification and should never be used in production. Secrets must be kept out of URLs (use headers instead).
